enrichment.short_description: Open-source event tracker that sends push notifications and can trigger actions when important product events happen.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, or 10,000 events/month free on the hosted cloud)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Operational.co is an open-source event tracking tool built for founders and small engineering teams who want visibility into what's happening inside their product without setting up a full analytics stack. Rather than tracking pageviews or funnels, it focuses on discrete operational events — a new signup, a failed webhook, a cron job finishing, a payment notification — and surfaces them on a live timeline.

The project was built by the team behind Swipekit, who use Operational internally to monitor their own production systems, and it now runs both as a hosted cloud service and as self-hosted software you deploy with Docker or on platforms like Render.com.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Operational is aimed at indie developers and small SaaS teams who want push notifications on their phone or desktop when something important happens in their backend, and who want the option to trigger a webhook-based action directly from that alert.

features.title: Live event timeline
features.description: See operational events — signups, cancellations, webhook receipts, cron runs — as they happen in a chronological feed.

features.title: Push notifications
features.description: Receive instant alerts for critical events on the Operational mobile/web app rather than checking dashboards manually.

features.title: Action buttons
features.description: Trigger a webhook directly from an event notification to kick off a follow-up workflow.

features.title: Event chaining
features.description: Link related events together so multi-step workflows and user paths can be understood as a single sequence.

features.title: JSON logging
features.description: Log structured JSON payloads alongside events for easier debugging and audit trails.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Deploy Operational yourself with Docker (or on Render.com) instead of using the hosted cloud version.

pros: Fully open source (AGPL-3.0) with a self-hosted option
Free tier on the hosted cloud (10,000 events/month)
Focused on operational events rather than generic product analytics, so signal is high
Push notifications keep founders informed without a dashboard habit
Built and battle-tested by the team on their own production apps
cons: Small, young project with a limited feature set compared to full observability platforms
Detailed hosted pricing tiers are not published on the public pricing page
Best suited to solo founders/small teams rather than large engineering organizations

faqs.question: Is Operational.co free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The software is open source (AGPL-3.0) and free to self-host. The hosted cloud version also includes a free tier of 10,000 events per month before usage-based billing applies.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Operational?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project provides Docker images and guides for self-hosting, including deployment on Render.com.

faqs.question: What kind of events does it track?
faqs.answer: Any operational event you send via its API — signups, payments, failed logins, cron job runs, webhook receipts, and similar backend occurrences.

faqs.question: Does it replace product analytics tools?
faqs.answer: No, it's designed as a complement — it focuses on operational alerting rather than user behavior analytics or funnels.

use_cases.title: Backend event alerting
use_cases.description: Get push notifications the moment a critical backend event occurs, like a failed payment or a cron job error.

use_cases.title: Workflow triggers from alerts
use_cases.description: Use action buttons on event notifications to fire a webhook and continue a workflow without opening a dashboard.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Operational.co is an open-source event tracker that sends push notifications and triggers webhooks for critical events in your product.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, dependency-free random password generator built in HTML and pure JavaScript with no trackers or cookies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: POMjs is a small, single-purpose random password generator written entirely in HTML and vanilla JavaScript by developer Joaquim Homrighausen. It loads no external resources, includes no analytics or cookies, and runs entirely client-side in the browser, making it easy to self-host as a static page or run from a personal server.
The project has been translated into several languages and is licensed under the GPLv2, with source code available on GitHub for anyone who wants to inspect, modify, or host their own copy.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It suits privacy-conscious users and self-hosters who want a lightweight, auditable password generator they fully control, rather than relying on a third-party website or browser extension.

features.title: Client-side generation
features.description: Passwords are generated entirely in the browser with pure JavaScript — nothing is sent to a server.

features.title: No cookies or trackers
features.description: The tool loads no external scripts, analytics, or tracking code of any kind.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: Available in English, Swedish, German, Hungarian, Dutch, French, and Finnish.

features.title: Easy to self-host
features.description: As a static HTML/JS project, it can be dropped onto any web server or personal domain in minutes.

pros: Fully open source and auditable (GPLv2)
No cookies, tracking, or external calls
Extremely lightweight and simple to deploy
Available in seven languages
cons: Very narrow scope — a password generator only, not a full password manager
No built-in storage/vault, browser extension, or mobile app
Minimal documentation and small maintainer community

faqs.question: Does POMjs store my passwords?
faqs.answer: No, it only generates passwords client-side in the browser; it has no storage or vault feature.

faqs.question: Is POMjs free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the GPLv2 license.

faqs.question: Can I run my own copy of POMjs?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source is on GitHub (joho1968/POMjs) and, being static HTML/JS, is straightforward to host on your own domain.

use_cases.title: Quick, private password generation
use_cases.description: Generate a strong random password without sending any data to a third-party server.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: POMjs is a free, open-source, dependency-free random password generator built in pure HTML and JavaScript with no cookies or trackers.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted redirect manager that lets you connect any domain and set up custom URL redirects in seconds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: re:Director is an open-source, self-hosted platform for managing URL redirects across multiple domains from a single dashboard. It's distributed as a Docker container and doesn't require an external database, so it can be stood up quickly on any Docker-capable host.
The project is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and provides a responsive web interface — including dark mode — for creating, filtering, pausing, and resuming redirects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's built for developers, marketers, and site owners who manage link redirects across several domains and want a centralized, self-hosted alternative to link-shortener or redirect SaaS products.

features.title: Multi-domain redirect management
features.description: Connect multiple domains and manage all their redirect rules from one place.

features.title: Configurable HTTP status codes
features.description: Choose between 301, 302, 307, and 308 redirects per rule.

features.title: Pause and resume redirects
features.description: Temporarily disable a redirect without deleting its configuration.

features.title: Advanced filtering
features.description: Filter and organize redirects by source, target, or status to manage large redirect sets.

features.title: Dockerized deployment
features.description: Ships as a Docker container with no external database dependency, simplifying self-hosting.

pros: Free and open source under Apache 2.0
Simple Docker deployment with no external database required
Supports multiple redirect status codes (301/302/307/308)
Dark mode and mobile-friendly interface
cons: Requires self-hosting and Docker familiarity — no official hosted version
Smaller community/project compared to established redirect and link-management SaaS tools
Limited public documentation on advanced configuration and scaling

faqs.question: Is re:Director free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: How do I deploy re:Director?
faqs.answer: It runs as a Docker container and doesn't require a separate external database, so it can be deployed with a single Docker command.

faqs.question: What redirect types does it support?
faqs.answer: It supports 301, 302, 307, and 308 HTTP redirect status codes.

use_cases.title: Centralized redirect management
use_cases.description: Manage redirect rules for many domains from one self-hosted dashboard instead of editing web server configs individually.

use_cases.title: Campaign link routing
use_cases.description: Set up and pause time-boxed marketing redirects without touching production DNS or server config.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: re:Director is a free, open-source, self-hosted tool for managing custom URL redirects across multiple domains via Docker.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, privacy-focused resume builder that can be used online or self-hosted with Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Reactive Resume, built by developer Amruth Pillai since 2019, is a free and open-source resume builder that lets people create, edit, and share resumes without handing their personal data to a third party. It's available at rxresu.me as a hosted web app, and the full source code can be self-hosted using Docker.

The project emphasizes privacy, portability, and customization: resumes are built from structured data and rendered through a choice of templates, and users can export their finished resume as a PDF or share it via a public link.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Reactive Resume suits job seekers who want a free, ad-free resume builder, as well as privacy-minded users and self-hosters who prefer to run their own instance rather than store resume data on a third-party server.

features.title: Multiple resume templates
features.description: Choose from a set of built-in templates and customize layout, colors, and typography.

features.title: Structured resume builder
features.description: Fill in sections like experience, education, and skills through a guided editor rather than a raw text document.

features.title: PDF export
features.description: Export a finished resume as a PDF, generated client-side in current versions without needing an external print service.

features.title: Shareable public links
features.description: Share a resume via a public URL instead of, or alongside, a downloaded file.

features.title: Self-hosting via Docker
features.description: Run your own private instance with a documented Docker Compose stack (PostgreSQL, plus optional storage service).

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Full source available on GitHub, built with React, TypeScript, and PostgreSQL/Drizzle ORM.

pros: Completely free with no paywalled features
Fully open source and self-hostable for full data control
Actively maintained with tens of thousands of GitHub stars
Modern, structured editor with multiple resume templates
Client-side PDF generation in recent versions simplifies self-hosting
cons: Self-hosting requires running PostgreSQL and other services via Docker
Template selection is smaller than some paid commercial resume builders
No official phone apps — web-based only

faqs.question: Is Reactive Resume really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the hosted version at rxresu.me is free to use, and the source code is open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Reactive Resume?
faqs.answer: Yes, official Docker-based self-hosting instructions and a Docker Hub image are provided in the documentation.

faqs.question: What does Reactive Resume use for PDF export?
faqs.answer: From version 5.1.0 onward, PDF generation runs client-side via @react-pdf/renderer, removing the need for an external Chromium/print service.

faqs.question: Who built Reactive Resume?
faqs.answer: It was created and is maintained by developer Amruth Pillai, with community contributions on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Building a job-search resume
use_cases.description: Create a structured, well-formatted resume using a guided editor and export it as a PDF.

use_cases.title: Private, self-hosted resume data
use_cases.description: Run a personal or team instance so resume data never touches a third-party server.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Reactive Resume is a free, open-source resume builder with multiple templates, PDF export, and self-hosting via Docker.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source HTML presentation framework for building interactive, web-based slide decks with themes and a JavaScript API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: reveal.js is an open-source HTML presentation framework created by Hakim El Hattab in 2011 and still actively maintained today. It turns standard HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into fully-featured slide decks that run in any web browser, with support for nested/vertical slides, Markdown-authored content, code syntax highlighting, LaTeX/math rendering, and PDF export.

Because presentations are just web pages, they can embed live web content via iframes, be styled with custom CSS, and be extended through a JavaScript API and plugin system. The core framework is free; its creator also offers Slides.com, an optional paid visual editor built on top of reveal.js for people who prefer a GUI over hand-written HTML/Markdown.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: reveal.js is aimed at developers, conference speakers, and technical writers who want version-controllable, code-driven presentations rather than binary slide files, and who are comfortable authoring in HTML or Markdown.

features.title: Markdown-authored slides
features.description: Write slide content in Markdown instead of hand-writing HTML for every slide.

features.title: Nested and vertical slides
features.description: Organize content into horizontal and vertical slide sequences for branching narratives.

features.title: Auto-Animate
features.description: Automatically animate matching elements between consecutive slides for smooth transitions.

features.title: Speaker notes view
features.description: A separate speaker view shows notes and a timer alongside the current and next slide.

features.title: Code and math support
features.description: Built-in syntax-highlighted code blocks and LaTeX/math rendering for technical talks.

features.title: PDF export
features.description: Export a full presentation to PDF for offline sharing or printing.

features.title: JavaScript API and plugins
features.description: Extend or control presentations programmatically through an extensive JS API and plugin system.

pros: Completely free, open-source, and self-hostable
Presentations are plain HTML/Markdown, so they're version-controllable in Git
Rich built-in features: auto-animate, speaker notes, math, code highlighting
Touch-optimized and works in any modern browser
Large, long-running open-source community (maintained since 2011)
cons: Requires comfort with HTML/CSS/Markdown — no built-in visual editor (that's the separate paid Slides.com)
Design customization requires CSS knowledge for a polished look
No native desktop or mobile app — purely web-based

faqs.question: Is reveal.js free?
faqs.answer: Yes, reveal.js itself is free and open source. Slides.com, a separate paid visual editor built on reveal.js, is optional.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use reveal.js?
faqs.answer: Basic HTML or Markdown knowledge is enough to build a presentation; JavaScript is only needed for custom behavior or plugins.

faqs.question: Can I export a reveal.js presentation to PDF?
faqs.answer: Yes, reveal.js includes built-in PDF export support.

faqs.question: Who maintains reveal.js?
faqs.answer: It was created by Hakim El Hattab in 2011 and continues to be maintained by him and the open-source community on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Conference and tech talks
use_cases.description: Build code-embedded, version-controlled slide decks for developer presentations.

use_cases.title: Interactive web-based decks
use_cases.description: Create presentations that embed live web content, demos, or interactive elements via iframes.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: reveal.js is a free, open-source HTML presentation framework for building interactive, code-driven slide decks in the browser.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source ad server for managing and delivering display, video, and mobile ad campaigns, with an optional paid hosted edition.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted download edition); hosted edition from $10/month

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Revive Adserver is a free, open-source ad serving platform used by publishers, ad networks, and advertisers to manage and deliver ad campaigns across websites, apps, and video players. It has been maintained since 2013 as a fork of the earlier OpenX Source project and is now developed under the Aqua Platform banner.

The software is available two ways: as a free download you self-host, or as a paid hosted edition (Revive Adserver Hosted) that removes the need to run and maintain the server infrastructure yourself, billed on a per-ad-request basis on top of a monthly plan minimum.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's built for publishers and ad networks who need to run their own ad-serving stack rather than relying solely on third-party ad exchanges, and who want fine-grained control over targeting, inventory, and reporting.

features.title: Campaign and inventory management
features.description: Manage advertisers, campaigns, banners, websites, and ad zones from a central admin interface.

features.title: Delivery targeting and capping
features.description: Set delivery rules including frequency capping, URL targeting, and geo-targeting for campaigns.

features.title: Performance statistics
features.description: Track impressions, clicks, conversions, CTR, and eCPM across campaigns and zones.

features.title: Multi-format ad support
features.description: Serve ads across websites, mobile apps, and video players.

features.title: Self-hosted or hosted deployment
features.description: Run the free download edition on your own servers, or use the paid Revive Adserver Hosted edition.

pros: Free, open-source download edition with full source on GitHub
Mature, long-running project (in active development since 2013)
Detailed targeting, frequency capping, and reporting features
Hosted edition available for teams who don't want to manage infrastructure
Positioned as GDPR-compliant/privacy-friendly ad serving
cons: Self-hosting the download edition requires server administration and maintenance
Hosted edition pricing is usage-based on top of a monthly minimum, which can add up at scale
Interface and workflow are dated compared to modern SaaS ad-tech tools

faqs.question: Is Revive Adserver free?
faqs.answer: The download edition is free and open source. A separate paid hosted edition is also available for those who don't want to self-host.

faqs.question: How much does the hosted edition cost?
faqs.answer: Hosted plans start at approximately $10/month for up to 1 million ad requests, with higher tiers (Premium, Super, Ultimate, Elite) for larger volumes, all billed at roughly $0.01 CPM.

faqs.question: What license does Revive Adserver use?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted software is open source, with source code published on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who maintains Revive Adserver?
faqs.answer: It's maintained by Aqua Platform and has been developed as an open-source project since 2013.

use_cases.title: In-house ad serving for publishers
use_cases.description: Serve and track ad campaigns directly on your own website without depending on a third-party ad network.

use_cases.title: Ad network campaign management
use_cases.description: Manage multiple advertisers, campaigns, and zones with targeting and reporting from a single system.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Revive Adserver is a free, open-source ad server for managing display, video, and mobile campaigns, with an optional paid hosted edition.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source SANE (Scanner Access Now Easy) API and daemon that let you share and access scanners over a network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SANE (Scanner Access Now Easy) is an open-source API and set of backend drivers, originally released in 1996, that provides standardized access to image scanners and similar capture devices, primarily on Unix and Linux systems. The SANE API itself is public domain, while the reference implementation is distributed under the GNU GPL.

The 'network scanning' capability referenced here comes from SANE's saned daemon and net backend: saned runs on the machine physically connected to a scanner and exposes it to permitted hosts on the network, while client machines use the net backend to access that remote scanner as if it were local. This lets a single scanner be shared across multiple computers on a network without dedicated network-scanner hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SANE is aimed at Linux/Unix users, self-hosters, and system administrators who want to share a scanner across a home or office network, or who need a standardized scanning API for building scanning frontends and applications.

features.title: Standardized scanner API
features.description: A common API (SANE) that scanning frontends can use regardless of the underlying scanner hardware.

features.title: saned network daemon
features.description: Exposes a locally connected scanner to other hosts on the network under access controls defined in saned.conf.

features.title: net backend
features.description: Lets client machines connect to a remote scanner shared via saned as though it were a local device.

features.title: Broad hardware support
features.description: Backend drivers exist for a wide range of flatbed, handheld, and other scanner hardware.

features.title: Standard protocol support
features.description: Includes backends for modern network scanning protocols such as AirScan/eSCL and WSD.

features.title: scanimage CLI frontend
features.description: A command-line frontend for scripting and automating scans.

pros: Free, open-source, and public-domain API with decades of active development since 1996
Lets a single scanner be shared across an entire network without proprietary network-scanner hardware
Broad backend support for many scanner models
Supports modern wireless/network scanner protocols (AirScan/eSCL, WSD) alongside legacy backends
cons: Primarily targets Unix/Linux; Windows support is limited and less officially maintained
Configuration (saned.conf, net.conf, access controls) is done via text files, not a graphical setup wizard
No official web-based UI — it's a system-level API/daemon, not an end-user app

faqs.question: What does SANE stand for?
faqs.answer: Scanner Access Now Easy — an open-source API for accessing image scanners and similar devices.

faqs.question: How does SANE support network scanning?
faqs.answer: The saned daemon runs on the host physically connected to the scanner and shares it with permitted network hosts; those clients use SANE's net backend to access it remotely.

faqs.question: Is SANE free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The SANE API is public domain, and the reference implementation is released under the GNU GPL.

faqs.question: Does SANE work on Windows?
faqs.answer: SANE primarily targets Unix/Linux; Windows support exists through community ports but is not the project's primary focus.

use_cases.title: Sharing a single scanner across a network
use_cases.description: Connect a scanner to one machine and let other computers on the network scan through it via saned.

use_cases.title: Building scanning applications
use_cases.description: Use the SANE API as a standardized backend for custom scanning software or frontends like xsane.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SANE is an open-source scanner API and daemon (saned) that lets you share and access image scanners over a network.

enrichment.short_description: Privacy-friendly, open-source online toolkit with over 50 string converters and formatters for developers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: string.is is an open-source, privacy-friendly web toolkit offering a collection of more than 50 text converters and formatters for developers — things like case conversion, encoding/decoding, hashing, and other everyday string transformations. Rather than trusting text to a proprietary third-party service, the project positions itself around transparency, publishing its source code openly.
It runs entirely as a web-based tool: paste or type text, pick a converter, and get the transformed output immediately, with no signup required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's aimed at developers who regularly need quick, one-off text transformations and prefer a privacy-conscious, open-source tool over closed third-party converter websites.

features.title: 50+ converters and formatters
features.description: A broad library of string transformation utilities covering common developer text-processing needs.

features.title: Privacy-friendly design
features.description: Built with a focus on not exposing user-submitted text to unnecessary tracking or third parties.

features.title: No-signup web interface
features.description: Use any converter directly in the browser without creating an account.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Source code is published publicly, letting users verify how their text is handled.

pros: Free and open source
Large library of over 50 text utilities in one place
Privacy-conscious design with published security/privacy documentation
No account or signup required to use it
cons: Limited public information on the underlying team or project history
As a general web utility, it may not cover highly specialized or niche string formats
No official self-hosting documentation confirmed for running your own instance

faqs.question: Is string.is free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free to use and open source.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use string.is?
faqs.answer: No, converters can be used directly in the browser without signing up.

faqs.question: How many converters does it offer?
faqs.answer: The site describes a collection of over 50 converters and formatters.

use_cases.title: Quick text/string conversion
use_cases.description: Convert case, encode/decode, or reformat text snippets during development without leaving the browser.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: string.is is a free, open-source, privacy-friendly toolkit offering 50+ string converters and formatters for developers.
